ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beet entry; no dedicated boiled-beetroot term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked beetroot as the closest available code; exact boiled preparation not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for boiled beetroot identified in published SNOMED CT releases.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled beetroot is approximately 80 g, equivalent to two to three medium slices or a small side portion.

nutrient_source.title: Beetroot, boiled — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14040001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry are set to -1 and documented in notes. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11080, Beets, cooked, boiled, drained) was consulted as a cross-reference for saturated fat and additional micronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Boiled beetroot GI of 64 (medium) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (64 × 8.16) / 100 ≈ 5.2, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (420 mg/100 g), low sodium (38 mg/100 g), and dietary nitrates that promote nitric oxide vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Boiled beetroot is beneficial for hypertension management: its high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports blood pressure regulation, and its naturally occurring dietary nitrates are converted to nitric oxide in vivo, producing clinically documented vasodilatory effects; prefer whole boiled beetroot over pickled or juiced forms to avoid added sodium or concentrated sugars.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 64) offset by low glycemic load per serving (GL ~5.2) and dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled beetroot has a moderate GI of 64 but a low glycemic load of approximately 5.2 per 80 g serving due to its relatively low carbohydrate density; individuals with type 2 diabetes may include it in moderate portions (80–100 g) as part of a balanced meal, while avoiding beetroot juice which removes fibre and concentrates sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high folate (80 mcg DFE/100 g), and nitrate-mediated endothelial benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled beetroot is cardioprotective: it contains negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, provides 80 mcg DFE folate per 100 g supporting homocysteine metabolism, and delivers dietary nitrates that improve endothelial function and reduce arterial stiffness per published cardiovascular evidence.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Betalain pigments (betacyanin, betaxanthin) with COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitory anti-inflammatory activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled beetroot contains betalain pigments that exhibit anti-inflammatory activity through inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes; steaming is preferred over boiling to better preserve betalain content, and pickled beetroot should be avoided due to high sodium which may exacerbate tissue swelling in rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled beetroot (Beta vulgaris) is a root vegetable prepared by cooking whole or sliced beetroot in boiling water until tender, then peeling and serving. It has a characteristic deep ruby-red colour from betalain pigments (betacyanin and betaxanthin), a mildly sweet and earthy flavour, and a soft, slightly firm texture. Boiling slightly raises the glycemic index compared to raw beetroot (GI ~64 vs ~61 raw) but the glycemic load per typical 80 g serving remains low at approximately 5.2, making it suitable for most dietary patterns including those managing blood glucose. Per 100 g, boiled beetroot provides 44 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 10.2 g carbohydrate (7.1 g sugars), 0.8 g dietary fibre, 38 mg sodium, and 420 mg potassium. It is an excellent source of dietary nitrates, which are converted to nitric oxide in the body and have clinically documented blood pressure-lowering and endothelial-protective effects. Folate content (approximately 80 mcg DFE/100 g) supports cardiovascular health through homocysteine metabolism. Boiled beetroot is widely consumed globally as a side vegetable, salad ingredient, or component of dishes such as borscht. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, it is considered beneficial for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis, and is acceptable in moderate portions for type 2 diabetes management.
common_user_queries: Is boiled beetroot good for blood pressure?
How many calories in boiled beetroot?
What is the glycemic index of boiled beetroot?
Is boiled beetroot good for heart health?
What nutrients are in boiled beetroot?
How much potassium in boiled beetroot?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040001, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid fractions and additional micronutrients cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB 11080 (Beets, cooked, boiled, drained). GI value of 64 confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.02 g), monounsaturated fat (0.02 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.05 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and micronutrients (vitamin C, A, B-vitamins, folate, D, E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this record; values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11080 (Beets, cooked, boiled, drained) as the closest approved secondary source. 2. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg: confirmed zero — beetroot is a plant food containing no cholesterol per HPB and USDA sources. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg: naturally zero as beetroot is a plant food containing no animal-derived vitamins. Trans fat is 0.0 g: naturally zero for a plain boiled vegetable. 3. Dietary fibre at 0.8 g/100 g is lower than some published values (USDA reports 2.0 g/100 g); the HPB value is retained as the primary source for this Singapore-context record. 4. GI of 64 is from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; glycemic load per 80 g serving = (64 × 8.16) / 100 ≈ 5.2 (low GL). 5. Boiled beetroot is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. Local name fields are set to null accordingly. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for boiled beetroot exists in published releases as of 2026-04-16.
